---
title: "Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)"
subtitle: "Minimum Wages and Employment: A Case Study of the Fast-Food Industry in New Jersey and Pennsylvania"
author: "Yunqing Zhao"
date: today
---


## Introduction
Card and Krueger (1994) study whether raising the minimum wage reduces employment in fast food restaurants. Their paper focuses on a policy change in New Jersey, where the state minimum wage increased from $4.25 to $5.05 in April 1992.

To study the effect of this policy, the authors compare fast food restaurants in New Jersey to similar restaurants in eastern Pennsylvania, where the minimum wage did not change. This gives them a treatment group and a control group.

This question matters because the standard textbook prediction is that a higher minimum wage should reduce employment. But Card and Krueger find something very different: employment does not fall, and may even rise slightly in New Jersey relative to Pennsylvania.

## Main Analysis
In this section, I replicate the main difference-in-differences result from Table 3 in Card and Krueger (1994). This is the key result of the paper since it compares how employment changed in New Jersey after the minimum wage increase relative to employment in eastern Pennsylvania, where the minimum wage did not change.

Following the paper, I measure employment using full-time equivalent employment (FTE), which is defined as the number of full-time workers plus one half of the number of part-time workers. I then compare average FTE employment before and after the policy change in the two states.

The goal is to reproduce the basic DID estimate reported in the paper and check whether the data lead to a result close to the published finding.

### Data preparation
```{python}
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Read fixed-width data
colspecs = [
    (0, 3),    # SHEET
    (4, 5),    # CHAIN
    (6, 7),    # CO_OWNED
    (8, 9),    # STATE
    (10, 11),  # SOUTHJ
    (12, 13),  # CENTRALJ
    (14, 15),  # NORTHJ
    (16, 17),  # PA1
    (18, 19),  # PA2
    (20, 21),  # SHORE
    (22, 24),  # NCALLS
    (25, 30),  # EMPFT
    (31, 36),  # EMPPT
    (37, 42),  # NMGERS
    (43, 48),  # WAGE_ST
    (49, 54),  # INCTIME
    (55, 60),  # FIRSTINC
    (61, 62),  # BONUS
    (63, 68),  # PCTAFF
    (69, 70),  # MEALS
    (71, 76),  # OPEN
    (77, 82),  # HRSOPEN
    (83, 88),  # PSODA
    (89, 94),  # PFRY
    (95, 100), # PENTREE
    (101, 103),# NREGS
    (104, 106),# NREGS11
    (107, 108),# TYPE2
    (109, 110),# STATUS2
    (111, 117),# DATE2
    (118, 120),# NCALLS2
    (121, 126),# EMPFT2
    (127, 132),# EMPPT2
    (133, 138),# NMGERS2
    (139, 144),# WAGE_ST2
    (145, 150),# INCTIME2
    (151, 156),# FIRSTINC2
    (157, 158),# SPECIAL2
    (159, 160),# MEALS2
    (161, 166),# OPEN2R
    (167, 172),# HRSOPEN2
    (173, 178),# PSODA2
    (179, 184),# PFRY2
    (185, 190),# PENTREE2
    (191, 193),# NREGS2
    (194, 196) # NREGS112
]

names = [
    "SHEET","CHAIN","CO_OWNED","STATE","SOUTHJ","CENTRALJ","NORTHJ","PA1","PA2","SHORE",
    "NCALLS","EMPFT","EMPPT","NMGERS","WAGE_ST","INCTIME","FIRSTINC","BONUS","PCTAFF","MEALS",
    "OPEN","HRSOPEN","PSODA","PFRY","PENTREE","NREGS","NREGS11","TYPE2","STATUS2","DATE2",
    "NCALLS2","EMPFT2","EMPPT2","NMGERS2","WAGE_ST2","INCTIME2","FIRSTINC2","SPECIAL2","MEALS2",
    "OPEN2R","HRSOPEN2","PSODA2","PFRY2","PENTREE2","NREGS2","NREGS112"
]

df = pd.read_fwf("Data_HW2/public.dat", colspecs=colspecs, names=names)

# Convert all columns to numeric
for col in df.columns:
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce")

df["state_name"] = df["STATE"].map({1: "NJ", 0: "PA"})

# Construct FTE employment including managers
df["fte_before"] = df["EMPFT"] + df["NMGERS"] + 0.5 * df["EMPPT"]
df["fte_after_raw"] = df["EMPFT2"] + df["NMGERS2"] + 0.5 * df["EMPPT2"]

df["meal_before"] = df["PSODA"] + df["PFRY"] + df["PENTREE"]
df["meal_after"] = df["PSODA2"] + df["PFRY2"] + df["PENTREE2"]

# Copy after-employment and apply Table 2 / Table 3 handling:
# permanently closed stores get employment = 0
df["fte_after"] = df["fte_after_raw"]
df.loc[df["STATUS2"] == 3, "fte_after"] = 0
df_t23 = df[df["STATUS2"].isin([1, 3])].copy()

df_t23.head()
```
### Replication some of Table 2
```{python}
# Table 2: selected descriptive statistics
table2_wave1 = (
    df.groupby("state_name")
      .agg(
          fte_wave1=("fte_before", "mean"),
          starting_wage_wave1=("WAGE_ST", "mean"),
          full_meal_price_wave1=("meal_before", "mean")
      )
      .reset_index()
)

# Wave 2 statistics
# For wages and prices in wave 2, use stores that answered the second interview
df_wave2_answered = df[df["STATUS2"] == 1].copy()

table2_wave2_nonemp = (
    df_wave2_answered.groupby("state_name")
      .agg(
          starting_wage_wave2=("WAGE_ST2", "mean"),
          full_meal_price_wave2=("meal_after", "mean")
      )
      .reset_index()
)


table2_wave2_emp = (
    df_t23.groupby("state_name")
      .agg(fte_wave2=("fte_after", "mean"))
      .reset_index()
)

# Merge into one compact table
table2_selected = (
    table2_wave1
    .merge(table2_wave2_emp, on="state_name")
    .merge(table2_wave2_nonemp, on="state_name")
)

table2_selected = table2_selected.round(3)
table2_selected
```
The replicated values are very close to those reported in Table 2 of the paper. In wave 1, employment levels are lower in New Jersey than in Pennsylvania, while starting wages are very similar across the two states. This suggests that the treatment and control groups are broadly comparable before the policy change.

In wave 2, starting wages increase in New Jersey relative to Pennsylvania, which is consistent with the minimum wage increase. At the same time, employment levels remain similar across the two states, which already hints that the policy did not lead to a clear decline in employment.

### Replication of Table 3
```{python}
# Table 3: rows 1-3, columns 1-3

table3 = (
    df_t23.groupby("state_name")[["fte_before", "fte_after"]]
      .mean()
      .reset_index()
)
table3 = table3.set_index("state_name").loc[["PA", "NJ"]].reset_index()

pa_before = table3.loc[table3["state_name"] == "PA", "fte_before"].iloc[0]
nj_before = table3.loc[table3["state_name"] == "NJ", "fte_before"].iloc[0]

pa_after = table3.loc[table3["state_name"] == "PA", "fte_after"].iloc[0]
nj_after = table3.loc[table3["state_name"] == "NJ", "fte_after"].iloc[0]

pa_change = pa_after - pa_before
nj_change = nj_after - nj_before

table3_rep = pd.DataFrame({
    "row": [
        "1. FTE employment before",
        "2. FTE employment after",
        "3. Change in mean FTE employment"
    ],
    "PA (i)": [pa_before, pa_after, pa_change],
    "NJ (ii)": [nj_before, nj_after, nj_change],
    "Difference, NJ-PA (iii)": [
        nj_before - pa_before,
        nj_after - pa_after,
        nj_change - pa_change
    ]
})

table3_rep = table3_rep.round(3)
table3_rep
did = nj_change - pa_change

print("NJ change:", round(nj_change, 3))
print("PA change:", round(pa_change, 3))
print("DID estimate:", round(did, 2))
```
The replicated results closely match those reported in Table 3 of the paper. In Pennsylvania, employment declined from about 23.33 to 21.17, a change of about -2.17. In contrast, employment in New Jersey increased slightly from about 20.44 to 21.03, a change of about 0.58.
The difference-in-differences estimate is about 2.75, which is nearly identical to the published value of 2.76. This indicates that employment in New Jersey increased relative to Pennsylvania after the minimum wage increase.

### Replication of Table 4, column (i)
```{python}
# Table 4 column (i): balanced sample with employment and wage data in both waves

df_t4 = df[
    (df["STATUS2"] == 1) &
    (df["fte_before"].notna()) &
    (df["fte_after_raw"].notna()) &
    (df["WAGE_ST"].notna()) &
    (df["WAGE_ST2"].notna())
].copy()

df_t4["delta_fte"] = df_t4["fte_after_raw"] - df_t4["fte_before"]

X = sm.add_constant(df_t4["STATE"])
y = df_t4["delta_fte"]

model_t4 = sm.OLS(y, X).fit()

coef_nj = model_t4.params["STATE"]
se_nj = model_t4.bse["STATE"]
ser = (model_t4.mse_resid) ** 0.5

table4_col1 = pd.DataFrame({
    "Statistic": [
        "NJ dummy coefficient",
        "Standard error",
        "Standard error of regression",
        "Number of observations"
    ],
    "Value": [
        coef_nj,
        se_nj,
        ser,
        int(model_t4.nobs)
    ]
})

table4_col1.round(2)
```
The regression results are very close to those reported in column (i) of Table 4. The coefficient on the New Jersey dummy is about 2.28, which is close to the published estimate of 2.33.

This coefficient represents the difference-in-differences estimate from a regression framework. It shows that employment in New Jersey increased relative to Pennsylvania after the minimum wage increase. The standard error and the standard error of the regression are also very similar to those reported in the paper.

## Something Additional
### The importance of DID analysis
The difference-in-differences (DID) approach is important in analysis since it focuses on changes over time rather than just comparing levels. In this paper, New Jersey is the treatment group and Pennsylvania is the control group.

The main idea is that DID helps remove factors that affect both states in the same way over time. For example, broader economic conditions, like a recession, would influence employment in both places. By comparing how employment changes in New Jersey relative to Pennsylvania, these shared time effects are removed.

DID also controls for fixed differences between the two states. Even if New Jersey and Pennsylvania started at different employment levels, those differences are not a problem because we are looking at changes instead of levels.

Overall, the DID design helps isolate the effect of the policy from other factors, making the results more convincing as a causal estimate.
